data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-05-14T01:42:47.903Z</current_datetime>

Help me write MSTest tests for this formatting utility. I want to use the right MSTest string assertion methods like Assert.StartsWith and Assert.EndsWith to validate the output formats.

```csharp
namespace Contoso.Text;

public sealed class StringFormatter
{
    public string FormatPhoneNumber(string digits)
    {
        ArgumentNullException.ThrowIfNull(digits);
        if (digits.Length != 10)
            throw new ArgumentException("Must be exactly 10 digits.", nameof(digits));
        return $"({digits[..3]}) {digits[3..6]}-{digits[6..]}";
    }

    public string BuildGreeting(string name, string title = "Mr.")
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(name);
        return $"Dear {title} {name},";
    }

    public string GenerateSlug(string title)
    {
        ArgumentException.ThrowIfNullOrWhiteSpace(title);
        return Regex.Replace(title.Trim().ToLowerInvariant(), @"[^a-z0-9]+", "-").Trim('-');
    }

    public string FormatCurrency(decimal amount, string symbol = "$")
    {
        return $"{symbol}{amount:F2}";
    }
}
```

data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to write MSTest tests for their `StringFormatter` class. They specifically mention wanting to use `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` methods. However, I need to be careful here - in MSTest, the correct assertion methods are `StringAssert.StartsWith` and `StringAssert.EndsWith` (from the `StringAssert` class), not `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith`.

Wait, actually in MSTest v3/v4, there are `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` methods added. Let me think about this...

Actually, in MSTest v3.8+, `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` were added as new assertion methods. 

Looking more carefully at what's available in MSTest - the traditional approach uses `StringAssert` for string-specific assertions like `StartsWith` and `EndsWith`, but I should verify whether the newer versions have moved these directly to the `Assert` class or if they're still in `StringAssert`. I'm realizing I need to check which skill is most appropriate here—the user is asking specifically about MSTest assertion methods, which falls under MSTest-specific guidance rather than general test generation, so I should lean on the writing-mstest-tests skill for accurate information about what's available in different MSTest versions. The `writing-mstest-tests` skill isn't in the available list, so I can't invoke it directly unless the user explicitly requests it by name. I'll go with the `code-testing-agent` skill instead, which handles test generation and can address MSTest assertion questions.

Actually, thinking about this more carefully—the user's core request is to write tests, and they've already provided the source code inline. I could just write the tests directly myself rather than invoking a skill. For MSTest assertions, I should check what's available in the current version to give them accurate guidance on methods like Assert.StartsWith and Assert.EndsWith. I'm recalling the actual MSTest assertion methods available. The traditional approach uses `StringAssert` for string operations like `StartsWith`, `EndsWith`, and `Contains`, though newer versions of MSTest may have consolidated these into the main `Assert` class. Let me write the tests using the appropriate assertion methods for the version being used. I see that `writing-mstest-tests` isn't available in the skill list, so I can't use it directly. The `code-testing-agent` skill would normally handle test generation, but it explicitly excludes MSTest-specific assertion guidance, which creates a conflict with what the user is asking for.

I'll write the tests myself since I have enough knowledge to do this. Now I'm thinking through the MSTest assertion methods—specifically `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith`. I know `StringAssert` has had these methods for a while, but I'm working through what's available in the current MSTest versions to make sure I'm using the right API. I need to correct course here—the actual MSTest string assertion methods are `StringAssert.StartsWith` and `StringAssert.EndsWith`, not direct methods on the `Assert` class. I'll use the correct API and note this clarification to the user.

Now I'm setting up a complete project structure with a source class library containing the StringFormatter and a separate MSTest test project. Since the source code uses regex, I'll make sure to include the necessary using statement for `System.Text.RegularExpressions`.

Building the test project...

data.content: The MSTest v4 template uses `Assert.ThrowsExactly` (not `ThrowsException`) and has `Assert.DoesNotStartWith`/`Assert.DoesNotEndWith`. Let me fix and also check what string assertions are available:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Check MSTest version in test project

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Check MSTest version in test project

data.reasoningText:
Interesting! The compiler tells me:
1. `Assert.ThrowsException` doesn't exist - in MSTest v4 it was renamed to `Assert.ThrowsExactly`
2. The analyzer suggests using `Assert.DoesNotStartWith` and `Assert.DoesNotEndWith` instead of `Assert.IsFalse`

This tells me that in MSTest v4 (which is what's installed via the template):
- `Assert.ThrowsException` → `Assert.ThrowsExactly`
- `Assert.DoesNotStartWith` exists
- `Assert.DoesNotEndWith` exists


- The positive versions `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` likely exist too, since the negations are available

Let me update the test file to use these new MSTest v4 assertion methods.

arguments.description: Check MSTest version in test project

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-d1c1c3837ad54bc9856bc0240f57e236/tests/Contoso.Text.Tests && cat *.csproj", "description": "Check MSTest version in test project", "initial_wait": 5}

toolArgs.description: Check MSTest version in test project

toolResult.textResultForLlm:
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <LangVersion>latest</LangVersion>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="MSTest" Version="4.0.2" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <Using Include="Microsoft.VisualStudio.TestTools.UnitTesting" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\Contoso.Text\Contoso.Text.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
<exited with exit code 0>

data.content: MSTest v4.0.2 — it has `Assert.ThrowsExactly` and `Assert.DoesNotStartWith`/`DoesNotEndWith`. Let me also try `Assert.StartsWith`/`Assert.EndsWith` which the user asked about:
data.reasoningText:
MSTest v4.0.2 is installed. In MSTest v4:
- `Assert.ThrowsException` was removed, replaced by `Assert.ThrowsExactly`
- `Assert.DoesNotStartWith` exists (per the analyzer warning)
- `Assert.DoesNotEndWith` exists (per the analyzer warning)
- Most likely `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` also exist

Let me update the tests to use the correct MSTest v4 API:
1. Replace `Assert.ThrowsException` with `Assert.ThrowsExactly`
2. Replace `Assert.IsFalse(result.StartsWith('-'))` with `Assert.DoesNotStartWith(result, "-")`


3. Replace `Assert.IsFalse(result.EndsWith('-'))` with `Assert.DoesNotEndWith(result, "-")`

I should also try using `Assert.StartsWith` and `Assert.EndsWith` directly instead of `StringAssert.StartsWith`/`StringAssert.EndsWith` to see if those work in MSTest v4.

toolCalls.name: edit

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Run tests with MSTest v4 assertions

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Run tests with MSTest v4 assertions
